title: CHAPTER II.
summary: Mr. Bennet was among the earliest of those who waited on Mr. Bingley. He had always intended to visit him, though to the last always assuring his wife that he should not go. The evening after the visit was paid she had no knowledge of it. It was then disclosed in the following manner.

title: CHAPTER III.
summary: Mr. Bingley was described as 'wonderfully handsome, extremelyagreeable, and, to crown the whole, he meant to be at the next assembly with a large party' Mrs. Bennet's five daughters attacked him with barefaced questions, ingenious suppositions, and distant surmises. Lady Lucas quieted her fears by starting the idea of his having ten thousand a year.

title: CHAPTER IV.
summary: Jane and Elizabeth Bingley met a young man, who was handsome and good-humoured. Mr. Bingley was endeared to Darcy by theeasiness, openness, and ductility of his temper. On the strength of Darcy’s regard, Bingley had the firmest reliance, and of his judgment the highest opinion.

title: CHAPTER V.
summary: Sir William Lucas had been formerly in trade in Meryton, where he had made a tolerable fortune. His neighbour, Charlotte Bennet, was a sensible, intelligent young woman, about twenty-seven. Jane Darcy saw Mr. Darcy speaking to Mrs. Long at Netherfield, but she said he seemed very angry at being spoken to.

title: CHAPTER VI.
summary: Jane Austen’s sister, Elizabeth, became an object of some proprietary interest in the eyes of Mr. Bingley. Jane and Bingley meet tolerably often, but never for many hours together. Eliza Lucas was teased by her friend, Mr. Darcy, for playing the violin.

title: CHAPTER VII.
summary: Catherine and Lydia Bennet lived in Longbourn, near Meryton. Their father was an attorney, and their mother had left her four thousand pounds. Jane Bennet received a note from her friend, Caroline Bingley, asking her to come and dine with her and her sisters. Her mother told her to go on horseback, as it was likely to rain.

title: CHAPTER VIII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet was still very poorly, and Elizabeth would not quit her at all, till late in the evening, when she had the comfort of seeing her asleep. She then said she would amuse herself, for the short time she could stay below, with a book. Mr. Hurst looked at her with astonishment. “With all my heart: I will buy Pemberley itself, if Darcy will sell it,” said Elizabeth Bennet. ‘I never met with anybody who delighted me so much.’

title: CHAPTER IX.
summary: Jane Bennet was too ill to be moved from Netherfield. She asked her mother to visit her, and form her own judgment of her situation. Lydia Bennet, the youngest of the Bennet daughters, was a favourite with her mother. She was a stout, well-grown girl of fifteen, with a fine complexion and good-humoured countenance.

title: CHAPTER X.
summary: Jane Austen's novel, Pride and Prejudice, was published in 1894. The novel is set in London in the early 1890s. The main character, Elizabeth Bennet, is recovering from a bout of pneumonia. The book was published by William Morrow and Co, and is published by Simon & Schuster.

title: CHAPTER XI.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet's sister and brother-in-law had dinner with the Bennet family at their home in Pemberley. The Bennet sisters were so engrossed in their conversation with Mr. Darcy that Elizabeth was left to her own devices. She was so tired of reading that she yawned and threw aside her book.

title: CHAPTER XII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet and her sister Jane were to stay at Netherfield till Tuesday. They were not welcomed home very cordially by their mother. Their father, though very laconic in his expressions of pleasure, was really glad to see them. Catherine and Lydia had information                for them of a different sort.

title: CHAPTER XIII
summary: A letter from Mr. Bennet to his late father-in-law reveals a plot to take control of his estate. The letter was from his cousin, Mr. Collins, who had been feuding with the Bennet family for years. The Bennet's wife, Jane, was furious at the prospect of losing her father's estate.

title: CHAPTER XIV
summary: Lady Catherine de Bourgh's attention to his wishes, and consideration for his comfort, appeared very remarkable. Mr. Collins was eloquent in her praise. He protested that he had never in his life witnessed such behaviour in a person of rank. “Protested that he never read novels.” Lydia gaped as he opened the volume; and, with monotonous solemnity, read three pages.

title: CHAPTER XV.
summary: Mr. Collins was not a sensible man, and the deficiency of nature had been but little assisted by education or society. He intended to marry; and in seeking a reconciliation with the Longbourn family he had meant to choose one of the daughters. The attention of every lady was soon caught by a young man, whom they had never seen before.

title: CHAPTER XVI.
summary: Mr. Wickham was the happy man towards whom almost every female eye wasturned. He was as far beyond them all in person, countenance, air, andwalk, as they were superior to the broad-faced stuffy uncle Philips. Lydia became very interested in the game, too eager in making bets and exclaiming after prizes, to have attention for anyone in particular.

title: CHAPTER XVII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet told Jane of her affair with Wickham the next day. Mr. Collins offered to pay for her to dance with Mr. Wickham. The Bennet sisters were delighted with the offer, but Mr. Darcy did not accept it. If there had not been a Netherfield ball to prepare for and talk of, the younger Miss Bennets would have been in a pitiable state.

title: CHAPTER XVIII.
summary: Elizabeth and Mr. Darcy were at a ball at Meryton. They stood for some time without speaking a word. He said not a word, and seemed desirous of changing the subject. He then turned to his partner and said,----“Sir William’s interruption has made me forget what we were talking about"

title: CHAPTER XIX.
summary: Mr. Collins made his declaration to Elizabeth Bennet the next day. He told her that he was going to leave Longbourn, Hertfordshire, to marry her. He said that he wanted to set the example of matrimony in his parish. Elizabeth would make no reply.

title: CHAPTER XX.
summary: Mr. Collins and Lizzy Bennet were engaged to be married. Her mother, Mrs. Bennet, told her husband to make Lizzy marry him. The couple were engaged for a year before they were married. Mr. Collins said he resented Lizzy's behaviour, but that he was resigned to it.

title: CHAPTER XXI.
summary: Charles Darcy left for London yesterday. His sister Caroline wrote to Elizabeth to tell her he would not return this winter. She wrote: 'I do not pretend to pretend toregret anything I shall leave in Hertfordshire except your society, my dearest friend' Lizzy: “Is it not clear enough? Does it not expresslydeclare that Caroline neither expects nor wishes me to be her sister?”

title: CHAPTER XXII.
summary: The Bennets were engaged to dine with the Lucases. Charlotte assured her friend of her satisfaction in being useful, and it amply repaid her for the little sacrifice of her time. ‘I’ve never been happier in my life.’ ‘It’s the only thing that has ever made me happy’

title: CHAPTER XXIII.
summary: Sir William Lucas was sent by his daughter toannounce her engagement to the family. Lady Lucas could not be insensible of triumph on being able to retort on having a daughter well married. Jane had sent Caroline an early answer to her letter, and was counting the days till she might reasonably hope to hear again. Elizabeth felt persuaded that no real confidence could ever subsist between them again.

title: CHAPTER XXIV.
summary: Jane Bennet received a letter from her brother's friend Miss Darcy. She was delighted to hear that he was fond of her, but her sister Elizabeth was not so sure. Her heart was divided between concern for her sister and resentment against all others. Mr. Bingley's name was scarcely ever mentioned between them.

title: CHAPTER XXV.
summary: Mrs. Gardiner, who was several years younger than Mrs. Bennet and Mrs. Philips, was a great favourite with her Longbourn nieces. “I never saw a more promising inclination; he was growing quite quiteattentive to other people, and wholly engrossed by her. Every time they met, it was more decided and remarkable”

title: CHAPTER XXVI.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet was warned by her aunt not to fall in love with Mr. Darcy. Her aunt's advice was a wonderful instance of advice being given without being resented. Mr. Collins returned into Hertfordshire soon after it had been quitted by Mrs. Bennet. Elizabeth Bennet's letters to Charlotte Lucas were as regular and frequent as they had ever been.

title: CHAPTER XXVII.
summary: Elizabeth Longbourn was sent to Hunsford by her father, Sir William Lucas, to visit her sister, Lady Catherine de Bourgh. The trip was a joy for Elizabeth, and for her aunt, Jane, but for Mr. Collins she was not happy to leave. Elizabeth was more grieved than astonished to hear, in reply to inquiries, that though Jane always struggled to support her, there were periods of dejection.

title: CHAPTER XXVIII.
summary: Lady Catherine de Bourgh was in Hertfordshire at the time of Elizabeth's visit. Lady Catherine's behaviour to Elizabeth was charming, according to Charlotte. Mr. Collins no sooner saw the two girls than he began to congratulate them on their good fortune. Charlotte explained that the whole party was asked to dine at Rosings the next day.

title: CHAPTER XXIX.
summary: Sir William de Bourgh and his wife were invited to dine with Lady Catherine and her family at Rosings. Sir William said he was not surprised by the invitation, but that he had expected it. Lady Catherine was a tall, large woman, with strongly-marked features, which might once have been handsome. She inquired into Charlotte’s domestic concerns familiarly and minutely.

title: CHAPTER XXX.
summary: Sir William stayed only a week at Hunsford, but his visit was long enough to convince him of his daughter’s being most comfortably settled. Elizabeth Bennet's first visit to Rosings was to meet Mr. Darcy. She was surprised to find that he had already been frequently seen by Miss Lucas and herself.

title: CHAPTER XXXI.
summary: Colonel Fitzwilliam asked Elizabeth Bennet to play for him. Lady Catherine Bennet said she would not be alarmed, as her sister could play so well. Colonel Fitz william asked Mr. Darcy how he behaved among strangers at a ball in Hertfordshire. He said he danced only four dances at the ball.

title: CHAPTER XXXII.
summary: Mr. Darcy asked Elizabeth Bennet if she was pleased with Kent. She replied that she was, and that it was a good match for her. He then said that she should not have said Mrs. Collins was settled near her family. He went away, and returned with Charlotte and her sister.

title: CHAPTER XXXIII.
summary: Elizabeth Darcy met Colonel Fitzwilliam more than once in the park at Hunsford. He asked her odd questions about her love of solitary walks, and her opinion of Mr. and Mrs. Collins’s happiness. He seemed to expect that whenever she came into Kent again she would be staying there too.

title: CHAPTER XXXIV.
summary: Elizabeth Darcy's letters to her sister Jane contained no actual complaint, nor was there any communication of present suffering. Mr. Darcy’s boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a sense of her sister's sufferings. Colonel Fitzwilliam called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her.

title: CHAPTER XXXV.
summary: Elizabeth Darcy received a letter from her former lover. The writer's sister was married to George Wickham, son of Pemberley's godson. He says he tried to prevent the marriage by warning Wickham of the evils of such a choice. He also says he did not tell Wickham about his sister's being in town.

title: CHAPTER XXXVI.
summary: Elizabeth Darcy received a letter from Mr. Darcy. She read with an eagerness which nearly left her power of comprehension. Her feelings as she read were scarcely to be defined. She put down the letter and re-read it. The letter from Colonel Fitzwilliam made Elizabeth feel depressed beyond anything she had ever known before.

title: CHAPTER XXXVII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet and her mother were invited to dinner by Lady Catherine Collins. Lady Catherine asked Elizabeth to stay a little longer at Rosings, but Elizabeth said she had only been there six weeks. Elizabeth's father, Colonel Darcy, felt the loss of friends very acutely, Lady Catherine said.

title: CHAPTER XXXVIII.
summary: Elizabeth was eager with her thanks and assurances of happiness. She had spent six weeks with great enjoyment. The pleasure of being with Charlotte, and the kind attention she had received, must make her feel obliged. Her home and her housekeeping, her parish and her poultry had not yet lost their charms.

title: CHAPTER XXXIX.
summary: Lydia, Kitty, Elizabeth, and Jane Bennet returned from a trip to Longbourn. The Bennet family had a party in the large dining-room, for almost the entire party was large, for the Lucasescame to meet. Elizabeth was determined to avoid seeing Wickham for as long as possible.

title: CHAPTER XL.
summary: Lizzy Bennet wrote to her sister asking for advice on how to deal with Wickham's letter to her. Jane Bennet told her that she should not make the letter public. Lizzy replied that Wickham was not authorized to make his communication public. She also said that she was unhappy with the way Wickham treated her.

title: CHAPTER XLI.
summary: Lydia Bennet was invited to Brighton by Mrs. Forster, the wife of the colonel of the regiment. Kitty and Lydia could not comprehend such hard-heartedness in any of the family. Elizabeth tried to be diverted by them, but all sense of pleasure was lost in shame. Lydia Bennet left her family early the next morning.

title: CHAPTER XLII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet's father married a woman whose weak understanding and illiberal mind put an end to all real affection for her. He was fond of the country and of books; from these tastes had arisen his principal enjoyments. To his wife he was very little otherwise indebted than as her ignorance and folly had contributed to his amusement. Lydia Forster's letters to her mother and sister were always long expected, and always very short.

title: CHAPTER XLIII.
summary: Elizabeth and her family visited the Darcy family home. They were surprised to see Mr. Darcy approaching them. He asked Elizabeth to introduce him to some of her friends. Elizabeth was overcome by shame and embarrassment at the perverseness of the meeting. She longed to know what at that moment was passing in his mind.

title: CHAPTER XLIV.
summary: Mr. Darcy and his family were at Longbourn to meet Elizabeth's sister, Georgiana. The lady's sensations remained a little in doubt, but that the gentleman was overflowing with admiration was obvious. Mr. Bingley expressed great pleasure in the certainty of seeing Elizabeth again. It was evident that he was very much in love with her.

title: CHAPTER XLV.
summary: Elizabeth Darcy's first visit to Pemberley was to meet Miss Bingley. Miss Darcy was accompanied by her niece, Georgiana, and two other ladies. They were seated in the saloon of the house, which had a northern aspect for summer. Elizabeth was delighted to hear her sister's opinion of her niece. "She would have said that he was a good man, and that she was proud of him"

title: Chapter XLVI.
summary: Lydia Darcy writes to her family, begging them to come to London as soon as possible. Denny expresses his belief that W. Darcy never intended to go there, or to marry Lydia at all. Colonel F. set off from B. to trace their route. He traced them easily to Clapham, but no farther.

title: CHAPTER XLVII.
summary: Lydia Wickham wrote to her friend Jane Bennet to tell her she was going to Longbourn. She had been visiting her mother, who had been taken ill. Colonel Forster came to Meryton, and saw the letter. He asked Elizabeth and Kitty to tell him more about the affair. Elizabeth Bennet was furious with Lydia for leaving her.

title: CHAPTER XLVIII.
summary: Mr. Gardiner left Longbourn on Sunday; his wife received a letter from him on Tuesday. He told them that on his arrival he had immediately persuaded his brother to come to Gracechurch Street. Elizabeth Bennet had never heard of Colonel Forster having any relations, except a father and mother, both of whom had been dead many years.

title: CHAPTER XLIX.
summary: Lydia Bennet will be married at 16, writes her uncle. Her mother is delighted, but her father does not know about it. Lydia Philips' sister Elizabeth is to be married. Elizabeth decides to go to Meryton to tell her sister's sister the news. She also plans other schemes to help her sister.

title: CHAPTER L.
summary: Five thousand pounds was settled by marriage articles on Mrs. Bennet and her children. Mr Bennet had never before supposed that, could Wickham be prevailed on to marry his daughter, it would be done with so little inconvenience to himself. Lydia Darcy was jealous of Mr. Darcy's esteem when she could no longer hope to be benefited by it.

title: CHAPTER LI.
summary: Lydia Bennet and her husband, Mr. Wickham, eloped with Elizabeth Bennet's mother, Mrs. Bennet’s sister, to Brighton. They stayed with the Bennet family for ten days, during which Lydia showed off her wedding ring and boasted about her husband. Lydia was exceedingly fond of Wickham; he was her dear Wickham on every occasion.

title: CHAPTER LII.
summary: Lydia Bennet and Mr. Darcy first met in London, where they met Wickham and Lydia's friend, Mrs. Younge. Mr Darcy tried to persuade Lydia to leave her disgraceful situation and return to her friends, but she was determined to stay. Mr. Bennet was obliged to leave the regiment on account of some debts of honour.

title: CHAPTER LIII.
summary: Lydia Bennet and her husband were to leave for Newcastle. The separation was likely to continue for at least a year. The loss of her daughter made Mrs. Bennet very dull for several days. The housekeeper at Netherfield had received orders to prepare for the arrival of her master.

title: CHAPTER LIV.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy met for the first time on Monday at Longbourn. The Bennet sisters were invited to a dinner at Netherfield by Mrs. Long and her nieces. The dinner was well-dressed and the partridges were well-cooked.

title: CHAPTER LV.
summary: Mr. Bingley visited the Bennet family a few days after his return from London. Mrs. Bennet invited him to dine with them, but he said he was engaged elsewhere. She invited him again, and he accepted with alacrity. He sat with them above an hour and was in remarkably good spirits. Jane could have no reserves from Elizabeth, where confidence would give her pleasure.

title: CHAPTER LVI.
summary: Lady Catherine de Bourgh visited the Bennet family a week after Bingley’s engagement with Jane had beenformed. She sat down without saying a word to Elizabeth Bennet, her youngest girl but one. Lady Catherine said that from their infancy, Darcy and his cousin had been intended for each other.

title: CHAPTER LVII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet was told by Lady Catherine that she was to break off her supposed engagement with Mr. Darcy. The letter was sent from Lady Catherine to Elizabeth's father, who was in his library at Netherfield. Elizabeth was shocked to hear that Lady Catherine had written to her daughter-in-law.

title: CHAPTER LVIII.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet and her sister Kitty were visiting Lady Catherine at Longbourn. They met Darcy at the Lucases' house, and Elizabeth told him how grateful she was for his kindness to her sister. Darcy wrote Elizabeth a letter of apology for what he had done to her. He said he had long been ashamed of the letter.

title: CHAPTER LIX.
summary: Lizzy Bennet is engaged to Mr. Darcy. She tells her sister Jane that she loves him more than she loves Bingley. Jane is incredulous and asks her if she can be happy with him. Lizzy says she is sure of its truth. Elizabeth Bennet's father was astonished when she told him of her choice of husband.

title: CHAPTER LX.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet wrote to her uncle and aunt to thank them for their congratulations on her marriage to Mr. Darcy. She also wrote to Lady Catherine to tell her how happy she was, and to ask her to come to Pemberley at Christmas. Lady Catherine had been rendered so enraged by the contents of her nephew’s letter that she was anxious to get away till the storm was blown over.

title: CHAPTER LXI.
summary: Elizabeth Bennet married Mr. Darcy and moved to Derbyshire. Kitty spent most of her time with her two elder sisters. Her father would never consent to her going to stay with Lydia. Mary was obliged to mix more with the world but could still moralize over every morning visit.
